news_summary: Recent health breakthroughs reveal that brain function can improve well into old age, innovative vaccines are preventing fatal overdoses, the vital role of grandparents in children's mental well-being is recognized, and new genetic discoveries shed light on rare neurological disorders.
news_summary_fr: Des avancées récentes dans le domaine de la santé montrent que les fonctions cérébrales peuvent continuer à s'améliorer jusqu'à un âge avancé, des vaccins innovants permettent d'éviter des surdoses mortelles, le rôle essentiel des grands-parents dans le bien-être mental des enfants est désormais reconnu, et de nouvelles découvertes génétiques apportent un éclairage sur des troubles neurologiques rares.
news_summary_es: Los últimos avances en el ámbito de la salud revelan que la función cerebral puede mejorar incluso en la vejez, que hay vacunas innovadoras que previenen las sobredosis mortales, que se reconoce el papel fundamental de los abuelos en el bienestar mental de los niños y que los nuevos descubrimientos genéticos arrojan luz sobre trastornos neurológicos raros.

articles.title: Your brain can keep improving into your 90s, study finds
articles.summary: A three-year study of nearly 4,000 adults ranging from age 19 to 94 found that brain health can improve at any age, challenging the common belief that mental sharpness must decline as we get older. Participants spent just a few minutes a day on brain-training activities, and researchers found measurable gains across multiple aspects of brain health, including thinking clarity, emotional well-being, and sense of purpose.
articles.content:
A new three-year study from researchers at The University of Texas at Dallas' Center for BrainHealth (CBH) suggests that getting older does not automatically mean losing mental sharpness. Instead, the findings indicate that brain health and cognitive abilities can continue to improve throughout life.

The research, published in Scientific Reports, a Nature journal, draws on data from The BrainHealth Project (BHP), an initiative launched by CBH in 2020 to better understand how people can strengthen and optimize brain health across the lifespan.

Researchers tracked 3,966 adults ranging in age from 19 to 94. This group represented roughly one-fifth of all BrainHealth Project participants. Over the course of three years, participants completed brief training activities that required only five to 15 minutes per day.

BrainHealth Index Tracks Changes Over Time

To evaluate changes in brain health and performance, the team used the BrainHealth Index (BHI), a patent-pending assessment developed by CBH researchers and first introduced in a 2021 pilot study.

The BHI is designed to detect both improvements and declines in brain health. It measures three primary areas: clarity, emotional balance, and connectedness to people and purpose.

"The BrainHealth Index brings together about 20 metrics, including validated gold-standard measures like the Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index and the Oxford Happiness Questionnaire, as well as tasks designed at the Center for BrainHealth to focus on more complex thinking skills," said Lori Cook MS'02, PhD'09, CBH director of clinical research and corresponding author of the Scientific Reports study. "This battery of assessments produces insights into individual brain health and change over time. Progress is measured by comparing results with participants' own earlier scores."

Cook, who also serves as an adjunct assistant professor in the School of Behavioral and Brain Sciences, said the findings challenge common assumptions about aging and cognition.

"Every brain is as unique as a fingerprint and has potential for growth," Cook said. "This study challenges the prevailing narrative of inevitable cognitive decline, suggesting instead that brain health can be proactively cultivated at any age."

Brain Improvement Seen Across All Ages

According to the researchers, positive changes were observed even among participants in their 80s, showing that efforts to improve brain health can be beneficial long before symptoms or disease appear and can remain effective later in life.

"For too long, we've operated under the outdated notion that we need to wait until something bad happens to our brains before we do anything for them," said Sandra Bond Chapman PhD'86, senior author of the study, CBH chief director and Dee Wyly Distinguished University Chair for BrainHealth. "This study reminds us that our brain is not defined by age -- it is defined by possibility."

One of the study's most notable findings involved participants who started with the lowest BrainHealth Index scores. This group experienced the largest improvements over time.

"Those who are starting at the lowest level appear to have the most opportunity for growth and may be coming in with more preexisting concerns," Cook said. "As such, they may be more motivated to invest the time needed to see more growth potential. But it is noteworthy that we saw measurable growth even in those entering as high performers."

Engagement Matters More Than Demographics

The researchers found that engagement was the strongest predictor of improvement. Factors such as age, gender, and education level did not determine whether participants experienced positive change.

However, Cook noted that the study population was not fully representative of the broader public. Most participants were white, female, and college educated.

"We have room to grow when it comes to representation for different demographic groups," she said. "We're working hard to increase representation, so we can be even more confident in how this generalizes to the population at large, especially communities typically underrepresented in research."

Cook, who has worked with Chapman for more than 25 years after beginning as a research assistant, said she values CBH's combination of clinical research, translational care, and community outreach. She also emphasized the importance of making scientific findings accessible and meaningful to the public.

"One piece that is so near and dear to my heart is helping people link neuroplasticity with self-agency," she said. "Brain health isn't just something we strive to maintain; we can actively shape it over time. Research like ours, which provides an objective measure of brain health that people can track over time, can only further boost public awareness."

Ongoing Brain Imaging Research

The BrainHealth Project continues to collect long-term data through additional studies and follow-up research. As part of that effort, approximately 400 participants from the Dallas area have undergone more than 1,200 brain scans at the Sammons BrainHealth Imaging Center.

"This unique imaging dataset provides an opportunity to look at neural metrics associated with the BHI and gives us the capacity to use periodic brain imaging to explore potential brain mechanisms associated with changes in brain health over time," Cook said.

Additional authors currently affiliated with CBH include medical science research director Dr. Jane Wigginton, who also serves as co-director of the Clinical and Translational Research Center and chief medical officer of the Texas Biomedical Device Center; Dr. Jeffrey Spence, director of biostatistics; Aaron Tate MA'18, director of emerging technology; Erin Venza MS'13, PhD'25, head of clinical operations; and Zhengsi Chang PhD'22, research scientist.

The study also included contributors from the Trinity College Institute of Neuroscience in Dublin, the University of California, Berkeley, and the Johns Hopkins University School of Medicine.

The BrainHealth Project receives support in part through private philanthropy, including funding from Sammons Enterprises Inc.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific study demonstrating that brain health and cognitive abilities can improve even into old age, challenging common assumptions about inevitable cognitive decline. The study involved a large, diverse age range of nearly 4,000 adults and showed measurable, positive brain health improvements from brief daily brain-training activities. This has broad implications for aging populations worldwide and promotes proactive brain health management, offering hope and empowerment for many people.
articles.personality_title: Study shows brain health can improve even in your 90s
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many people believe that our brains naturally lose sharpness as we get older. Scientists have long thought that mental skills decline with age and cannot be improved later in life.

**What happened** – Researchers at the University of Texas at Dallas studied nearly 4,000 adults aged 19 to 94 over three years. Participants spent just a few minutes daily on brain-training activities. Using a special BrainHealth Index, scientists measured changes in thinking clarity, emotional balance, and sense of purpose. They found that brain health improved at all ages, including in people in their 80s and 90s.

**Impact** – This study challenges the idea that aging means inevitable mental decline. It shows that anyone, no matter their age, can boost their brain health with simple daily exercises. People who started with lower brain scores improved the most, proving that it’s never too late to see benefits. The research also found that how much people engaged in training mattered more than their age, gender, or education.

**What's next step** – The BrainHealth Project will continue studying participants and use brain scans to better understand how brain changes happen. Researchers also want to include more diverse groups of people to make sure the findings apply to everyone. This ongoing work could help develop better ways to keep brains healthy throughout life.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A large study reveals that simple daily brain exercises can improve mental abilities well into old age, showing brain health can grow at any time in life.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une étude montre que la santé du cerveau peut s'améliorer même à 90 ans
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Beaucoup pensent que notre cerveau perd naturellement de sa vivacité avec l'âge. Les scientifiques croyaient longtemps que les capacités mentales déclinent et ne peuvent plus s'améliorer en vieillissant.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de l'Université du Texas à Dallas ont étudié près de 4 000 adultes âgés de 19 à 94 ans pendant trois ans. Les participants ont consacré quelques minutes par jour à des exercices cérébraux. Grâce à un indice spécial, le BrainHealth Index, ils ont mesuré des changements dans la clarté de la pensée, l'équilibre émotionnel et le sens du but. Ils ont constaté une amélioration de la santé du cerveau à tous les âges, même chez les personnes dans la 80e et 90e année.

**Impact** – Cette étude remet en question l'idée que vieillir signifie un déclin mental inévitable. Elle montre que chacun, quel que soit son âge, peut améliorer la santé de son cerveau avec de simples exercices quotidiens. Les personnes avec des scores cérébraux plus faibles ont progressé le plus, prouvant qu'il n'est jamais trop tard pour en bénéficier. L'étude a aussi montré que l'engagement dans les exercices compte plus que l'âge, le sexe ou le niveau d'éducation.

**Prochaine étape** – Le BrainHealth Project va continuer à suivre les participants et utiliser des scanners cérébraux pour mieux comprendre comment le cerveau évolue. Les chercheurs veulent aussi inclure des groupes plus diversifiés pour que les résultats s'appliquent à tous. Ce travail permettra peut-être de mieux protéger la santé cérébrale tout au long de la vie.

**Une phrase clé** – Une grande étude révèle que des exercices cérébraux simples quotidiens peuvent améliorer les capacités mentales même à un âge avancé, prouvant que la santé du cerveau peut progresser à tout moment de la vie.
articles.personality_title_es: Estudio muestra que la salud del cerebro puede mejorar incluso a los 90 años
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchas personas creen que el cerebro pierde agudeza de forma natural con la edad. Los científicos han pensado durante mucho tiempo que las habilidades mentales disminuyen y no pueden mejorar en la vejez.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Texas en Dallas estudiaron a casi 4,000 adultos de 19 a 94 años durante tres años. Los participantes dedicaron solo unos minutos diarios a actividades para entrenar el cerebro. Usando un índice especial llamado BrainHealth Index, midieron cambios en la claridad del pensamiento, el equilibrio emocional y el sentido de propósito. Encontraron mejoras en la salud cerebral a todas las edades, incluso en personas de 80 y 90 años.

**Impacto** – Este estudio desafía la idea de que envejecer significa una pérdida mental inevitable. Muestra que cualquier persona, sin importar la edad, puede mejorar la salud de su cerebro con ejercicios simples diarios. Quienes empezaron con puntajes bajos mejoraron más, demostrando que nunca es tarde para beneficiarse. También descubrieron que el nivel de compromiso fue más importante que la edad, el género o la educación.

**Próximo paso** – El BrainHealth Project continuará estudiando a los participantes y usará escáneres cerebrales para entender mejor cómo cambia el cerebro. Los investigadores también quieren incluir grupos más diversos para que los resultados sean aplicables a todos. Este trabajo puede ayudar a desarrollar mejores formas de mantener el cerebro sano durante toda la vida.

**Una frase clave** – Un gran estudio revela que ejercicios cerebrales simples y diarios pueden mejorar las habilidades mentales incluso en la vejez, mostrando que la salud del cerebro puede crecer en cualquier momento de la vida.
articles.image_prompt: An intricate, warm-toned painting of a glowing, evolving neural network shaped like a vibrant tree with interconnected branches and roots, each branch representing clarity, emotional balance, and connectedness, set against a softly lit abstract backdrop symbolizing a lifelong journey of growth and cognitive improvement.

articles.title: New fentanyl vaccine blocks deadly overdoses before they start
articles.summary: A new experimental vaccine developed by Scripps Research could offer a powerful new way to prevent fentanyl overdoses by stopping the drug before it reaches the brain. Rather than targeting only fentanyl itself, the vaccine trains the immune system to recognize a broad range of fentanyl-related designer drugs, including some of the most dangerous variants.
articles.content:
Fentanyl has become one of the deadliest drugs in the United States. Each year, fentanyl and related synthetic opioids are responsible for more deaths than car crashes and gun violence combined. In high doses, these drugs disrupt normal brain function and can suppress the signals that control breathing, often leading to fatal overdoses. Although medications can reverse an overdose, they must be administered quickly to be effective.

Researchers at Scripps Research are now exploring a very different strategy. Instead of treating an overdose after it happens, they have developed an experimental vaccine designed to stop fentanyl from reaching the brain in the first place.

The findings, published in the Journal of Medicinal Chemistry, indicate that the vaccine may provide protection not only against fentanyl itself but also against a wide range of fentanyl-related "designer drugs." These modified versions are often created to increase potency or help manufacturers avoid detection and regulation.

"What this research shows us is that we don't have to keep playing catch-up with every new synthetic designer drug that emerges," says senior author Kim Janda, the Ely R. Callaway, Jr. Professor of Chemistry at Scripps Research. "By training the immune system to recognize the entire fentanyl class -- not just individual structures -- we can stay ahead of illicit drug traffickers."

A New Approach to Fentanyl Prevention

Scientists have spent years working on vaccines that trigger the production of antibodies capable of binding to fentanyl in the bloodstream before it can affect the brain. Janda's laboratory has previously developed vaccine candidates targeting both fentanyl and heroin.

However, most vaccine designs rely on the drug itself, or a molecule that closely resembles it, to train the immune system. That approach presents two major challenges. First, the drugs involved are highly regulated, making research and development more difficult. Second, the immune response tends to be highly specific, meaning it may recognize only the exact drug used in the vaccine.

"The way the fentanyl landscape is evolving, the black-market drug makers are constantly coming up with new versions to skirt regulations and avoid detection in standard screenings," says Janda. "We need countermeasures that are going to work against all these future variants at once, not just one at a time."

Testing an Unconventional Vaccine Design

In earlier research, Janda's team developed a modified form of fentanyl that maintained its pain-relieving effects while eliminating many of the drug's harmful side effects. For the new study, the researchers investigated whether a related molecule could serve as the foundation for a vaccine.

The molecule shared some characteristics with fentanyl but had a fundamentally different core structure.

"When we started testing this molecule as a vaccine component, we honestly didn't know if it would work," says Arran Stewart, a research associate in the Janda lab and first author of the study. "The conventional wisdom says that to get the immune system to recognize fentanyl, you have to use something that looks like fentanyl. We were doing the opposite."

To test the idea, the team attached the modified molecule to a carrier protein and administered four vaccine doses to mice over an eight-week period.

The results surprised the researchers. Rather than requiring an exact match to fentanyl's structure, the immune system generated antibodies that recognized a broader molecular signature shared by many fentanyl-related compounds.

Broad Protection Against Fentanyl Variants

When the scientists evaluated the antibodies against multiple fentanyl designer drugs, the vaccine demonstrated the broad protection they had hoped to achieve.

The antibodies strongly recognized fentanyl as well as several dangerous variants, including carfentanil, China White, acetylfentanyl and furanylfentanyl. At the same time, they did not bind to commonly used medical opioids such as morphine, oxycodone, remifentanil and alfentanil.

The protective effects were also evident in animal testing. Mice that received the vaccine maintained nearly normal breathing even after being given fentanyl doses that would typically cause severe respiratory depression.

Researchers also found that fentanyl levels in the brains of vaccinated mice were approximately 70% lower than in mice that had not received the vaccine.

Potential Future Applications

The vaccine must still undergo clinical trials to determine whether it is safe and effective in people. Even so, Janda believes the platform could eventually help protect individuals enrolled in substance abuse recovery programs and others who face a high risk of fentanyl exposure.

"The public health potential here is significant," says Janda. "But so is the lesson that we can design vaccines that recognize an entire drug class, not just a singular drug."

The study, titled "Redefining Drug Immune Recognition: A Radically Reconfigured Molecular Architecture Enables Broad Fentanyl-Class Protection," was authored by Janda, Stewart, Lisa Eubanks, Bin Zhou and Rachel Steinhardt, all of Scripps Research.

The work was supported by the Shadek Family Foundation.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a significant scientific breakthrough: an experimental vaccine that could prevent fentanyl overdoses by training the immune system to recognize a broad range of fentanyl-related drugs. This has the potential to save many lives given the scale of fentanyl-related deaths, and it represents a novel, proactive approach to a major public health crisis. The research is detailed, focused, and has broad societal impact.
articles.personality_title: New vaccine offers broad protection against deadly fentanyl overdoses
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Fentanyl is a powerful drug that has caused many deaths in the United States by slowing down breathing and causing overdoses. Current medicines can reverse overdoses but must be given very quickly to work.

**What happened** – Scientists at Scripps Research have created an experimental vaccine that trains the immune system to stop fentanyl and many related drugs from reaching the brain. Instead of targeting just fentanyl, the vaccine works against a whole group of fentanyl-like drugs, including dangerous new versions made to avoid detection. In tests on mice, the vaccine helped keep breathing normal even after high doses of fentanyl.

**Impact** – This vaccine is different because it can protect against many types of fentanyl drugs, not just one kind. This is important because illegal drug makers often change fentanyl’s structure to create new versions. The vaccine’s ability to recognize a broad group of fentanyl drugs means it could help prevent overdoses more effectively than treatments that work only after an overdose happens.

**What's next step** – The vaccine still needs to be tested in humans to make sure it is safe and works well. If successful, it could be used to protect people at high risk of fentanyl exposure, such as those in recovery programs or communities affected by fentanyl.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new vaccine shows promise to prevent fentanyl overdoses by training the immune system to block many dangerous fentanyl-related drugs before they harm the brain.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouveau vaccin offre une protection large contre les overdoses mortelles de fentanyl
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le fentanyl est une drogue puissante qui cause de nombreux décès aux États-Unis en ralentissant la respiration et provoquant des overdoses. Les médicaments actuels peuvent inverser une overdose, mais ils doivent être administrés très rapidement.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des scientifiques de Scripps Research ont créé un vaccin expérimental qui apprend au système immunitaire à empêcher le fentanyl et de nombreuses drogues similaires d’atteindre le cerveau. Au lieu de cibler seulement le fentanyl, le vaccin agit contre un groupe entier de drogues apparentées, y compris de nouvelles versions dangereuses fabriquées pour éviter la détection. Lors de tests sur des souris, le vaccin a aidé à maintenir une respiration normale même après de fortes doses de fentanyl.

**Impact** – Ce vaccin est différent car il peut protéger contre plusieurs types de drogues à base de fentanyl, pas seulement une seule. Cela est important car les trafiquants modifient souvent la structure du fentanyl pour créer de nouvelles versions. La capacité du vaccin à reconnaître un large groupe de drogues signifie qu’il pourrait mieux prévenir les overdoses que les traitements qui interviennent seulement après une overdose.

**Prochaine étape** – Le vaccin doit encore être testé sur des humains pour vérifier sa sécurité et son efficacité. S’il réussit, il pourrait être utilisé pour protéger les personnes à haut risque d’exposition au fentanyl, comme celles en programmes de rétablissement ou dans les communautés touchées.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un nouveau vaccin promet de prévenir les overdoses de fentanyl en apprenant au système immunitaire à bloquer plusieurs drogues dangereuses apparentées avant qu’elles n’atteignent le cerveau.
articles.personality_title_es: Nueva vacuna ofrece protección amplia contra sobredosis mortales de fentanilo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El fentanilo es una droga muy fuerte que ha causado muchas muertes en Estados Unidos al disminuir la respiración y provocar sobredosis. Los medicamentos actuales pueden revertir una sobredosis, pero deben usarse muy rápido.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos del Scripps Research crearon una vacuna experimental que enseña al sistema inmunológico a bloquear el fentanilo y muchas drogas similares antes de que lleguen al cerebro. En lugar de atacar solo el fentanilo, la vacuna funciona contra un grupo amplio de drogas relacionadas, incluyendo nuevas versiones peligrosas hechas para evitar ser detectadas. En pruebas con ratones, la vacuna ayudó a mantener la respiración normal incluso con dosis altas de fentanilo.

**Impacto** – Esta vacuna es diferente porque protege contra muchos tipos de drogas relacionadas con el fentanilo, no solo una. Esto es importante porque los traficantes cambian la estructura del fentanilo para crear nuevas versiones. La capacidad de la vacuna para reconocer muchas drogas significa que podría prevenir sobredosis mejor que los tratamientos que solo actúan después de una sobredosis.

**Próximo paso** – La vacuna todavía debe probarse en humanos para asegurar que sea segura y efectiva. Si funciona, podría ayudar a proteger a personas con alto riesgo de exposición al fentanilo, como quienes están en programas de recuperación o en comunidades afectadas.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Una nueva vacuna promete prevenir sobredosis de fentanilo entrenando al sistema inmunológico para bloquear muchas drogas peligrosas relacionadas antes de que dañen el cerebro.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a protective shield formed by intertwining antibodies gently blocking dark, abstract molecular shapes resembling fentanyl and its variants from reaching a simplified, glowing brain silhouette, set against a soft, natural-toned background.

articles.title: Why grandparents matter more than ever for children's mental health
articles.summary: A child psychologist says grandparents are more important than ever as youth mental health challenges continue to rise. He argues that children need supportive relationships, meaningful conversations, and a sense of purpose—not just pressure to achieve. Grandparents can help by listening, encouraging, and creating positive experiences that strengthen emotional resilience.
articles.content:
With more than 40% of U.S. teenagers reporting ongoing feelings of sadness or hopelessness, one child psychologist believes grandparents may be an important part of the solution.

Kenneth Barish, Ph.D., Clinical Professor of Psychology at Weill Cornell Medicine, says the decline of extended family involvement has helped fuel what the U.S. Surgeon General has described as a continuing crisis in child and adolescent mental health.

"We did not evolve to raise children with as little extended family and community support as most American parents have now," says Dr. Barish, a Fellow of the American Psychological Association. "Children need grandparents, and they always have."

In his new book, The Art and Science of Parenting and Grandparenting, Dr. Barish draws on 40 years of clinical experience, as well as findings from neuroscience, child development studies, and educational programs, to argue that grandparents can play a meaningful role in helping families navigate today's parenting challenges.

Why Purpose Matters for Children's Well-Being

According to Dr. Barish, grandparents can help counter a cultural trend that has increasingly emphasized individual achievement over community and connection.

"Over several decades, America has increasingly become a society of I, not We. In many families and communities, preoccupation with individual achievement has eroded the values of kindness and caring in the lives of our children," he explains.

Research has linked intense achievement pressure to elevated rates of anxiety, depression, and substance abuse, particularly in affluent communities. Dr. Barish argues that children need a stronger sense of purpose that extends beyond personal accomplishments.

"Individual achievement alone is a fragile source of motivation and effort, with a high cost in anxiety and stress," Dr. Barish writes. "Helping others promotes a greater balance in children's emotional lives."

Evidence reviewed by psychologist Jane Piliavin found that helping others is associated with higher self-esteem, lower rates of depression, reduced school dropout rates, improved immune function, and even longer life expectancy.

To encourage these benefits, Dr. Barish recommends volunteering as a family and regularly talking with children, beginning at a young age, about kindness, empathy, and understanding other people's feelings and needs.

He explains: "These conversations strengthen a child's sense of meaning and purpose. They are just as important as making sure kids have done their homework and correcting their mistakes, maybe more."

How Grandparents Support Children's Mental Health

Dr. Barish says grandparents offer more than practical support for parents. They also provide what he describes as 'molecules of emotional health', small but meaningful moments of encouragement, attention, and understanding that help strengthen children's 'emotional immune systems'.

"A child's confident expectation that someone will listen and understand is the best protection against the emotional pathogens they will experience throughout their childhood. "More than anything else, children need someone in their life who listens, who helps them feel less alone, and who teaches them that problems can be solved, relationships can be repaired, and bad feelings do not last forever," Dr. Barish explains.

He also highlights the importance of play, shared enjoyment, and showing genuine enthusiasm for children's interests and goals. These positive interactions can help build emotional resilience and strengthen family relationships.

The Hidden Harm of Excessive Criticism

One of the most common parenting challenges Dr. Barish encounters is not too much praise, but too much criticism.

In his clinical work, he has found that well-intentioned family members often underestimate the negative effects of frequent criticism.

"The most common problem I see in my work with families is not too much praise, but too much criticism," Dr. Barish states.

"Criticism does not motivate children to work harder. Instead, frequent criticism breeds resentment and defiance, and undermines children's initiative and effort."

At the same time, he notes that not all praise is equally beneficial. Drawing on Carol Dweck's concept of a "growth mindset," he encourages adults to focus praise on effort and learning rather than innate ability.

"Praise effort, not intelligence or talent. Praise learning, not grades."

Building Confidence Through Conversation

Dr. Barish acknowledges that raising children often involves managing difficult behavior. In his book, he outlines 21 principles designed to encourage cooperation, based on both scientific research and decades of clinical experience.

Among his recommendations are involving children in collaborative problem solving and giving them opportunities to 'reset', an approach he believes works better than punishment.

Ultimately, Dr. Barish argues that helping children thrive depends less on teaching specific skills and more on fostering emotional strength, confidence, and meaningful relationships.

Dr. Barish explains: "Helping our children and grandchildren succeed in life is less about teaching skills and more about having conversations; less about earning rewards and more about learning to cope with painful feelings; less about clearing a path to success and more about strengthening an inner feeling of confidence and pride. Our children will then work harder, bounce back more quickly, show more caring and kindness toward others, and pursue interests with greater enthusiasm, commitment, and sense of purpose."
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a meaningful, evidence-based approach to improving children's mental health by emphasizing the vital role grandparents play in providing emotional support and fostering resilience. It addresses a broad societal issue—the rising youth mental health crisis—and offers a hopeful, actionable solution grounded in psychology and neuroscience.
articles.personality_title: Grandparents play a key role in supporting children's mental health
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many young people in the U.S. are feeling sad or hopeless, with over 40% of teenagers reporting these feelings. Experts say this is part of a larger mental health crisis among children and teens.

**What happened** – Dr. Kenneth Barish, a child psychologist and professor at Weill Cornell Medicine, explains that grandparents can help improve children's mental health. He wrote a book sharing ideas from his 40 years of work and research. Dr. Barish says grandparents provide important emotional support by listening, encouraging, and spending quality time with children.

**Impact** – Dr. Barish points out that kids today face a lot of pressure to succeed, which can cause anxiety and stress. Grandparents can help by focusing on kindness, empathy, and teaching children to care for others. This helps children feel they have a purpose beyond just achievements. Positive moments with grandparents build emotional strength, making kids more confident and better able to handle problems.

**What's next step** – Families are encouraged to involve grandparents more in children's lives through shared activities and conversations about feelings and kindness. Dr. Barish also suggests praising children’s effort and learning, not just results, and using teamwork to solve problems instead of punishment.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Grandparents offer vital emotional support that helps children build confidence and resilience amid today’s mental health challenges.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les grands-parents jouent un rôle clé dans le soutien de la santé mentale des enfants
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – De nombreux jeunes aux États-Unis se sentent tristes ou désespérés, plus de 40 % des adolescents déclarant ressentir ces sentiments. Les experts expliquent que cela fait partie d’une crise plus large de la santé mentale chez les enfants et les adolescents.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le Dr Kenneth Barish, psychologue pour enfants et professeur à Weill Cornell Medicine, explique que les grands-parents peuvent aider à améliorer la santé mentale des enfants. Il a écrit un livre partageant des idées issues de ses 40 années de travail et de recherche. Le Dr Barish dit que les grands-parents apportent un soutien émotionnel important en écoutant, en encourageant et en passant du temps de qualité avec les enfants.

**Impact** – Le Dr Barish souligne que les enfants d’aujourd’hui subissent beaucoup de pression pour réussir, ce qui peut causer de l’anxiété et du stress. Les grands-parents peuvent aider en se concentrant sur la gentillesse, l’empathie et en apprenant aux enfants à prendre soin des autres. Cela aide les enfants à sentir qu’ils ont un but au-delà des réussites. Les moments positifs avec les grands-parents renforcent la force émotionnelle, rendant les enfants plus confiants et mieux préparés à gérer les problèmes.

**Prochaine étape** – Les familles sont encouragées à impliquer davantage les grands-parents dans la vie des enfants par des activités partagées et des conversations sur les sentiments et la gentillesse. Le Dr Barish recommande aussi de féliciter les efforts et l’apprentissage des enfants, pas seulement les résultats, et d’utiliser le travail d’équipe pour résoudre les problèmes plutôt que la punition.

**Message clé en une phrase** – Les grands-parents offrent un soutien émotionnel essentiel qui aide les enfants à développer confiance et résilience face aux défis actuels de la santé mentale.
articles.personality_title_es: Los abuelos juegan un papel clave en el apoyo a la salud mental de los niños
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchos jóvenes en Estados Unidos se sienten tristes o desesperanzados, con más del 40 % de los adolescentes reportando estos sentimientos. Los expertos dicen que esto forma parte de una crisis mayor de salud mental en niños y adolescentes.

**Qué pasó** – El Dr. Kenneth Barish, psicólogo infantil y profesor en Weill Cornell Medicine, explica que los abuelos pueden ayudar a mejorar la salud mental de los niños. Escribió un libro compartiendo ideas de sus 40 años de trabajo e investigación. El Dr. Barish dice que los abuelos brindan apoyo emocional importante escuchando, animando y pasando tiempo de calidad con los niños.

**Impacto** – El Dr. Barish señala que hoy los niños enfrentan mucha presión para tener éxito, lo que puede causar ansiedad y estrés. Los abuelos pueden ayudar enfocándose en la bondad, la empatía y enseñando a los niños a cuidar a los demás. Esto ayuda a los niños a sentir que tienen un propósito más allá de los logros. Los momentos positivos con los abuelos fortalecen la fuerza emocional, haciendo a los niños más seguros y mejor preparados para enfrentar problemas.

**Próximo paso** – Se anima a las familias a involucrar más a los abuelos en la vida de los niños mediante actividades compartidas y conversaciones sobre sentimientos y amabilidad. El Dr. Barish también recomienda elogiar el esfuerzo y el aprendizaje de los niños, no solo los resultados, y usar el trabajo en equipo para resolver problemas en lugar de castigos.

**Frase clave** – Los abuelos ofrecen un apoyo emocional vital que ayuda a los niños a construir confianza y resiliencia frente a los desafíos actuales de la salud mental.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of two intertwined golden threads glowing softly against a muted natural background, symbolizing the strong, nurturing bond between grandparents and grandchildren that weaves emotional support, resilience, and shared purpose into children’s mental well-being.

articles.title: A hidden gene finally explains this rare neurological disorder
articles.summary: Scientists have uncovered a surprising new genetic cause of a rare movement disorder after analyzing nearly 3,000 patients with conditions affecting coordination and muscle control. The team identified mutations in a gene called CD99L2, previously linked only to the immune system, and showed that it plays an essential role in keeping nerve-cell communication running smoothly.
articles.content:
Even with today's advanced DNA sequencing technologies, the underlying genetic causes of many rare movement disorders remain unknown. Researchers in Germany have now uncovered an important new clue. By analyzing 2,811 people with ataxia, hereditary spastic paraplegia, and dystonia, scientists identified harmful variants in a gene called CD99L2 as the cause of X-linked spastic ataxia.

The discovery, published in Nature Communications, helps explain a previously unsolved neurological disorder and offers new insight into how certain neurodegenerative diseases develop.

Researchers Link CD99L2 to Rare Neurological Disease

Before this study, CD99L2 was mainly recognized for its role in the immune system. No neurological function had been established for the gene.

Using a combination of genome-wide genetic analysis and laboratory experiments in cells, the research team demonstrated that CD99L2 is also essential for communication pathways within nerve cells. Their findings revealed that the gene plays a critical role in maintaining normal neuronal signaling.

How the Gene Affects Brain Cell Function

Scientists at Ruhr University Bochum found that the protein produced by CD99L2 works as an activating partner for CAPN1, a calcium-dependent protease already known to be involved in hereditary spastic paraplegia and ataxia.

"Disease-causing variants lead to disrupted production of the CD99L2 protein in the cell and prevent its interaction with CAPN1," explains Dr. Jonasz Weber. "Patients' cells also showed specific disruptions of synaptic processes."

According to the researchers, defects in CD99L2 reduce the activation of CAPN1. This, in turn, disrupts important neuronal signaling pathways, providing a likely explanation for the movement-related symptoms seen in affected patients.

Combining Genetics and Neuroscience

The findings highlight the value of combining genetic testing with functional studies of how genes operate inside cells.

"Our results show that genetic diagnostics and functional neuroscience are not mutually exclusive areas," says Weber. "Only when both disciplines work closely together can a reliable disease mechanism be derived from a genetic variant."

Identifying CD99L2 as a disease-causing gene could improve genetic diagnosis for people with rare movement disorders. It also provides researchers with new information about the biological processes involved in neurodegeneration.

What Is Spastic Ataxia?

Spastic ataxia is a group of rare neurodegenerative disorders characterized by problems with movement coordination (ataxia) together with spastic paralysis. The symptoms result from damage affecting the cerebellum and motor pathways within the central nervous system.

The age at which symptoms appear and the progression of the disease can vary widely depending on the underlying genetic cause.

The large-scale genetic analysis of the patient cohort was carried out in Tübingen under the supervision of Dr. Tobias Haack. Functional studies of the newly identified disease gene were led by Dr. Jonasz Weber and colleagues at the Department of Human Genetics at Ruhr University Bochum.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough identifying a new genetic cause for a rare neurological disorder, which improves understanding of neurodegenerative diseases and can enhance genetic diagnosis and patient care. The discovery has broad implications for medical research and treatment of rare movement disorders, providing tangible benefits to affected patients and advancing neuroscience.
articles.personality_title: New gene discovery sheds light on rare movement disorder
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long struggled to find the genetic causes behind many rare movement disorders, which affect how people control their muscles and movements. These disorders include ataxia, spastic paraplegia, and dystonia, which cause problems with coordination and muscle control.

**What happened** – Researchers in Germany studied the DNA of nearly 3,000 patients with these movement disorders. They found mutations in a gene called CD99L2, which was previously known only for its role in the immune system. Their experiments showed that CD99L2 is important for nerve cells to communicate properly, and mutations in this gene cause a rare disorder called X-linked spastic ataxia.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it reveals a new cause for a rare neurological disease that was not understood before. It connects a gene from the immune system to brain cell function, explaining why patients experience problems with movement. This knowledge can help doctors diagnose patients more accurately and guide future research on how these diseases develop.

**What's next step** – Scientists will continue studying how CD99L2 works in nerve cells and how its defects lead to disease. This could lead to better genetic tests and possibly new treatments for people with spastic ataxia and related disorders.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Researchers have found that mutations in the gene CD99L2 disrupt nerve cell communication, causing a rare movement disorder and opening new paths for diagnosis and research.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle découverte génétique éclaire un trouble moteur rare
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques ont longtemps cherché à comprendre les causes génétiques de nombreux troubles rares du mouvement, qui affectent la coordination et le contrôle des muscles. Ces troubles incluent l’ataxie, la paraplégie spastique et la dystonie.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs en Allemagne ont analysé l’ADN de près de 3 000 patients atteints de ces troubles. Ils ont découvert des mutations dans un gène appelé CD99L2, auparavant connu uniquement pour son rôle dans le système immunitaire. Leurs expériences ont montré que ce gène est essentiel à la communication entre les cellules nerveuses, et que ses mutations causent une maladie rare appelée ataxie spastique liée à l’X.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle révèle une nouvelle cause d’une maladie neurologique rare jusque-là incomprise. Elle relie un gène du système immunitaire au fonctionnement des cellules cérébrales, expliquant les troubles du mouvement chez les patients. Cela peut aider les médecins à mieux diagnostiquer et orienter la recherche.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont poursuivre l’étude du rôle de CD99L2 dans les cellules nerveuses et comment ses défauts provoquent la maladie. Cela pourrait améliorer les tests génétiques et ouvrir la voie à de nouveaux traitements.

**Une phrase clé** – Des chercheurs ont découvert que des mutations du gène CD99L2 perturbent la communication nerveuse, causant un trouble moteur rare et ouvrant de nouvelles perspectives pour le diagnostic et la recherche.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo descubrimiento genético aclara un trastorno del movimiento raro
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante mucho tiempo, los científicos han intentado encontrar las causas genéticas de muchos trastornos raros del movimiento, que afectan el control de los músculos y la coordinación. Estos incluyen ataxia, paraplejía espástica y distonía.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores en Alemania analizaron el ADN de casi 3,000 pacientes con estos trastornos. Encontraron mutaciones en un gen llamado CD99L2, antes conocido solo por su papel en el sistema inmunológico. Sus experimentos mostraron que este gen es esencial para la comunicación entre las células nerviosas y que sus mutaciones causan un trastorno raro llamado ataxia espástica ligada al cromosoma X.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo es importante porque revela una nueva causa para una enfermedad neurológica rara que no se entendía bien. Conecta un gen del sistema inmunológico con la función cerebral, explicando los problemas de movimiento en los pacientes. Esto puede ayudar a los médicos a diagnosticar mejor y a guiar futuras investigaciones.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos seguirán estudiando cómo funciona CD99L2 en las células nerviosas y cómo sus defectos causan la enfermedad. Esto podría mejorar las pruebas genéticas y abrir camino a nuevos tratamientos.

**Una frase clave** – Los investigadores descubrieron que mutaciones en el gen CD99L2 alteran la comunicación nerviosa, causando un trastorno del movimiento raro y abriendo nuevas vías para el diagnóstico y la investigación.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of an intricate neural network gently illuminated from within, where golden threads symbolizing the CD99L2 gene weave seamlessly into glowing pathways connecting stylized neuron-like shapes, with a subtle interplay of two interlocking puzzle pieces representing the partnership between CD99L2 and CAPN1 proteins, all set against a soft, natural background of muted earth tones.
